title: Hidden periodicities allow the prediction of locked particle motions on quasicrystalline surfaces
authors: Seemant Mishra, Artem Ryabov, Philipp Maass
abstract: Motion of particles across quasicrystalline surfaces exhibits peculiar features due to the presence of long-range order without translational periodicity. Under time-periodic forcing, this motion can become locked in directions thatn deviate strongly from the mean driving direction. We show that for surface potentials with a quasicrystalline pattern of minima generated by a superposition of plane waves, particle trajectories are nonperiodic, yet their mean direction and speed are determined by hidden periodic potentials. The lattice vectors of these underlying potentials define characteristic velocities that dictate both directional and speed locking. The particle motion does not synchronize with the driving, and it is possible for the mean speed to remain nonlocked even in directionally locked states. These findings are demonstrated using a model directly amenable to experimental realization.
summary: This paper analyzes particle movement on quasicrystalline surfaces driven by oscillating forces. It demonstrates that despite the aperiodicity of the surface potential, the mean direction and speed of the particles become locked to values determined by hidden periodic symmetries within the potential structure. The findings are significant because they reveal underlying deterministic order governing transport in complex quasiperiodic media.
main_problem: The study investigates particle motion on quasicrystalline surfaces, aiming to predict directional and speed locking when subjected to time-periodic external driving forces.
main_result: Particle mean velocities can lock into specific directions dictated by the lattice vectors of underlying hidden periodic potentials, even though the overall surface potential lacks translational periodicity. Furthermore, this directional locking is often accompanied by a predictable locking of the mean particle speed.
method: The analysis uses theoretical modeling based on Langevin dynamics for overdamped motion over a quasicrystalline potential derived from plane wave superpositions. Results are confirmed via numerical simulations and geometric analysis linking observed plateaus to underlying lattice symmetries.
model_or_system: The system models particles moving across surfaces with potentials $U(\mathbf{r})$ exhibiting quasicrystalline order, generated by summing multiple plane waves. The dynamics are governed by a Langevin equation incorporating both DC and time-periodic AC driving forces.
key_observables: Mean particle velocity ($ ar{\mathbf{v}}$), the angle of mean motion ($	heta_v$), and the mean speed ($| ar{\mathbf{v}}|$).
important_parameters: The ratio of characteristic speeds to the inverse period ($1/	au$) of the driving force; noise strength ($D$).
main_assumptions: The particle dynamics are well-described by the overdamped Langevin equation, and the underlying potential structure is accurately captured by plane wave superpositions.
figures_summary: Figures demonstrate directional locking as plateaus in $	heta_v$ vs $	heta$, correlating these directions to lattice vectors $\mathbf{d}^{(ij)}_{m_1 m_2}$. Speed locking is shown via linear dependence of $ ar{v}$ on $1/	au$.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical model and dynamics, analyzes directional locking by relating observed plateaus in mean velocity angle to lattice vectors derived from hidden periodic potentials. It then extends this analysis to show that speed locking is also predicted by these same underlying symmetries.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete example of how symmetry principles (hidden periodicity) can govern macroscopic transport properties (locking) in complex, non-periodic media, which has implications for understanding energy dissipation and directed motion in engineered materials or biological systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Ten-fold rotationally symmetric potential U(r) generated by five sinusoidal plane waves with wave vectors kj [Eqs. (2), (10)]. Its minima are indicated by green bullets. (b) Bragg diffraction pattern of the quasicrystalline lattice of potential minima. (c) Hidden periodic potential u(12)(r) [Eq. (3)]. Minima of u(12)(r) form an oblique lattice spanned by primitive vectors a(12) 1 and a(12) 2 orthogal to the wavevec- tors k2 and k1, respectively [Eq. (4)].

FIG. 2. Directional locking of particle motion when driven by the force Fdc + Fac(t) [Eqs. (12), (13)] across the potential U(r) of Fig. 1(a). In (a)-(d), Fdc = 10(cos θ, sin θ). (a) Angle θv between mean particle velocity ¯v and x-axis as a function of the mean driving angle θ for (F ac x , F ac y ) = (0, 0) (black line), and for ac driving with (F ac x , F ac y ) = (20, 7) and 1/τ = 3.9 (blue triangles), 4.4 (green diamonds), and 4.9 (purple squares). The red circles are for (F ac x , F ac y ) = (0, 20) and 1/τ = 7. Dashed horizontal lines indicate robust locking directions. (b) Color plot with logarithmic scale of relative counts (frequencies) of θv for θ ∈[0◦, 36◦). For each θ we have...

FIG. 3. Directional locking without and with speed locking for Fdc = 14, (F ac x , F ac y ) = (2, 2), and 1/τ = 7. For the θ- interval between the vertical dotted lines, the mean velocity (¯vx, ¯vy) is varying, while θv = arctan(¯vy/¯vx) stays constant. Between the vertical dashed and dotted lines, speed locking goes along with directional locking.

FIG. 4. Mean particle speed ¯v as a function of the ac-driving frequency 1/τ for (a) mean driving angle θ = 7◦and force parameters Fdc = 10, (F ac x , F ac y ) = (20, 7) as in Fig. 2(a), and (b) θ = 0◦, Fdc = 12, and (F ac x , F ac y ) = (20, 20). Circles represent results from simulations and lines have slopes the- oretically predicted when applying the conjecture of hidden periodicities. For the labeling of the lines, see Eqs. (7)-(9).
